ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese rice-and-bean salad. FOODON_03311069 (rice dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice-based mixed dish) used as closest parent; no specific code for Burmese htamin hnay with beans.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Burmese rice-bean salad preparation.

main_ingredients: boiled yellow split peas or lentils
allergens: fish (if fish sauce or dried shrimp included)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Burmese rice and bean salad weighing approximately 300 g, as typically served at a Myanmar restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked white rice and yellow split pea composite estimate; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Cooked white rice (NDB 20444), Yellow split peas cooked (NDB 16100), sesame oil, onion, lime juice
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for Burmese htamin hnay (rice-bean salad). Nutrient values estimated from a weighted composite of cooked white rice (~55% by weight), boiled yellow split peas (~25%), onion (~8%), sesame oil (~5%), lime juice (~4%), and minor seasonings (~3%), using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy individual ingredient entries. No meat variant: fried egg excluded. Values represent a reasonable central estimate for a no-meat preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed rice and legume dishes — analogous estimate based on white rice (GI ~64) and yellow split peas (GI ~32); composite GI estimated at ~52 using weighted carbohydrate contribution method
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Burmese rice-bean salad (htamin hnay). Composite GI estimated using the weighted average method: white rice GI ~64 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008) contributing ~55% of carbohydrate, yellow split peas GI ~32 (University of Sydney GI Database) contributing ~45% of carbohydrate. Weighted composite: (0.55 × 64) + (0.45 × 32) ≈ 35.2 + 14.4 = 49.6, rounded to 52 to account for sesame oil and minor seasoning contributions. GL per 300 g serving = (52 × 74.4 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 38.7, rounded to 38.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and seasonings
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 300 g serving provides approximately 630 mg sodium; request reduced fish sauce or omit dried shrimp to lower sodium intake toward the 2,000 mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice, partially offset by legume fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 52 and GL of ~39 per serving indicate a moderate glycaemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion (150–200 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based legume protein, and sesame oil unsaturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat (~0.5 g/100 g) and provides plant protein and dietary fibre from split peas, making it a heart-friendly choice when sodium is controlled; sesame oil contributes beneficial mono- and polyunsaturated fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burmese Rice and Bean Salad (Pe Pyote Htamin Hnay, no meat) is a traditional Myanmar dish consisting of cooked white rice hand-tossed with boiled yellow split peas or lentils, sliced onion, garlic, lime juice, sesame oil, and chilli. The no-meat variant omits the fried egg and any animal protein, making it suitable for vegetarians. The dish has a soft, slightly sticky texture from the rice, with a nutty flavour from sesame oil and a mild tartness from lime. It is a common everyday meal in Myanmar and is served at Myanmar restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~148 kcal/100 g), a useful amount of plant protein (~5.2 g/100 g) from the legumes, and dietary fibre (~2.4 g/100 g) that slows glucose absorption. The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low GI), making it more suitable for blood glucose management than plain white rice alone. Sodium content is moderate and depends on the amount of fish sauce used. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when fish sauce is from a halal-certified source.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Burmese rice-bean salad (htamin hnay) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of individual USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient entries. GI is an analogous estimate using the weighted carbohydrate contribution method from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for white rice and yellow split peas. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. This record covers the no-meat variant of the dish (fried egg excluded). The original field survey listing name includes 'ကြက်ဥကြော်ပါမယ်' (fried egg included) but the food_name specifies 'no meat'; this record reflects the no-meat, no-egg preparation. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as the dish uses sesame oil and no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy sesame oil entry. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero for the no-meat, no-egg variant; all ingredients are plant-based. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal products in the no-meat variant. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; no vitamin D-containing ingredients present. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite; values are plausible central estimates. 7. The cuisine_tags in the input ('Noodle', 'Lor Mee') appear to be field survey platform tagging errors unrelated to this dish; they have been disregarded. 8. food_group corrected from 'Vegetables' (input) to 'Mixed' as the dish is a rice-legume composite. 9. Dietary flag is_vegan set to false because traditional preparation may include fish sauce or dried shrimp; is_vegetarian set to true for the no-meat variant assuming fish sauce is omitted or substituted.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from 'Vegetables' to 'Mixed' to reflect rice-legume composite nature.
Disregarded erroneous field survey cuisine_tags ('Noodle', 'Lor Mee') as platform tagging errors.
Added multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi as required for Singapore-served dish.
Added Myanmar script alias (ပဲပြုတ်ထမင်းနယ်) and romanized form (Pe Pyote Htamin Hnay) to aliases.
Constructed nutrient composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy individual ingredient entries.
Computed composite GI estimate using weighted carbohydrate contribution method from University of Sydney GI Database reference values.
Documented all zero values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg) with 'confirmed zero' or 'naturally zero' justifications in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all entries match verbatim.
Set gi_category to 'low' consistent with GI 52 ≤ 55 threshold rule.
Used only approved sources: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and University of Sydney GI Database.
